behaviors.description: Cron parser rejects invalid expressions (month 13, day 32, malformed syntax) with descriptive error messages.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is parser/validator logic at one local boundary. REAL-INFRA: no OS/network/subprocess needed. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: higher levels are disqualified because behavior text is strictly about input validation + error text, not module handoff or infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Call the parser with invalid cron strings and assert structured errors include specific field/syntax diagnostics (e.g., invalid month/day token position). This catches real failures in validation and error construction without requiring module wiring or external systems.

behaviors.description: Next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard cron expressions, including last day of month and nearest weekday.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: core failure is date-calculation algorithm correctness. REAL-INFRA: deterministic time math can run in-process. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: Integration is not required unless behavior explicitly requires parser->calculator handoff; text focuses on calculator correctness itself.
behaviors.test_description: Given fixed start instants and cron specs, assert the next N timestamps exactly match expected sequences, including month-end rollover and nearest-weekday rules. This directly catches calculation defects at the function/object boundary.

behaviors.description: Overlap detector identifies schedule pairs that fire within a configured minimum gap and reports conflicting windows.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: real failure often appears at module boundary (schedule expansion output consumed by overlap detector + window reporter). Integration-brake: (a) no real OS/network/subprocess required, (b) no AI output dependence, (c) no multi-agentic chain, so not System/Agentic/Workflow. Rejection gate: Unit is too low when bug is in handoff between run-time generation and overlap comparison.
behaviors.test_description: Wire parser/schedule-expander and overlap detector together in-process. Feed two cron schedules plus a 5-minute gap and assert returned conflict windows match expected timestamps/ranges. This catches interface/normalization mismatches that isolated unit tests can miss.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling handles DST spring-forward gap and fall-back ambiguity for a 2:30 AM job.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is typically in interaction between scheduler logic and timezone/DST resolution rules, not just one pure function. Integration-brake: (a) no required subprocess/network/OS primitive in behavior text, (b) no AI dependence, (c) no multi-agentic chain. Rejection gate: System was considered, but behavior text only requires correct timezone-aware scheduling semantics; this is testable with in-process timezone library + known DST fixtures.
behaviors.test_description: Run scheduler components end-to-end in-process with a real timezone database (e.g., America/New_York fixtures) across concrete DST dates. Assert spring-forward 2:30 handling follows policy (skip/shift) and fall-back 2:30 ambiguity resolves consistently per engine rules. This catches DST normalization and module-wiring defects.

behaviors.description: When next run is in the past after downtime, engine applies missed-run policy (skip vs backfill) correctly.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure appears when calculator output, persisted last-run state, policy evaluator, and scheduler decision logic interact. Integration-brake: (a) no mandatory OS/network/subprocess, (b) no AI output dependence, (c) no agentic sequence. Rejection gate: Unit is insufficient if policy is correct in isolation but wrong after state handoff from scheduling components.
behaviors.test_description: Wire scheduling engine modules with an in-memory repository/clock fixture. Simulate downtime so computed next run is stale, then assert resulting actions for each policy: skip schedules only future run; backfill emits required missed executions within configured limits. Validates cross-module policy application under realistic state flow.
